name: Codon
summary: Shajii et al. (MIT/Exaloop, PLDI 2023): Python-syntax compiler producing standalone native binaries via LLVM, without CPython runtime overhead. Imposes static type inference on a Python subset, monomorphises generics, emits LLVM IR with no heap-tag dispatch on hot paths. 10–100× speedups on numeric/string workloads.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Shajii et al.'s Codon (MIT/Exaloop, PLDI 2023) is a Python-syntax compiler that produces standalone native binaries via LLVM, without CPython runtime overhead. Where Cinder/Pyston/Pyjion and the CPython JIT work *with* Python's reference-counted dynamic semantics, Codon abandons them: it imposes static type inference on a Python subset, monomorphises generic code at compile time, and emits LLVM IR with no heap-tag dispatch on hot paths.

Reported speedups are 10–100× on numeric and string-heavy workloads, with C-competitive performance where Codon's inferred types are tight enough.

sections.title: Python dialect and AOT compilation
sections.content:
The architectural choice is "compile a Python *dialect*, not the language itself": full CPython-extension semantics, dynamic class modification, and ad-hoc reflection are not supported. In exchange, Codon offers domain-extensibility through a plugin SDK — the same compiler infrastructure backs Seq (computational genomics) and other domain-specific Codon dialects, each adding domain-typed primitives without forking the toolchain.

Distinct from Numba, which JIT-compiles selected functions inside an otherwise standard CPython process; Codon replaces CPython entirely.

sections.title: Whole-program typing and monomorphization
sections.content:
Codon's performance comes from compile-time reasoning that dynamic Python typically does not allow: whole-program or broad-scope type understanding plus monomorphization of generic code at compile time. Each generic function is specialized at every used type instantiation; the resulting LLVM IR has no heap-tag dispatch on hot paths.

This is the same compilation strategy MLton uses for Standard ML and Roc uses for its lambda-set-driven defunctionalization — a typed Python dialect joins the whole-program-monomorphization compiler family.

sections.title: Compatibility trade-offs
sections.content:
Codon makes the compatibility/performance trade-off explicit. It keeps much of Python's surface familiarity while giving up enough dynamism to support a far more aggressive compilation pipeline.

Status (as of 2026-04): production-stable; Exaloop ships Codon under a source-available license with commercial deployments in computational biology and high-frequency data processing. The lesson generalises: a typed Python dialect plus whole-program AOT is a different point in the Python-acceleration design space from any JIT, and pays off when partial Python compatibility is acceptable.
